refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Verification method: automated telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and initiate safe state transition within defined timeout.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link status and availability metrics to the Safety Manager and Site Manager.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The network infrastructure shall provide redundancy and failover mechanisms to achieve target availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual independent communication paths (primary and backup) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously and switch to backup on primary failure.
DP-B: Deploy a single high-reliability communication link with error correction and retransmission; rely on network provider SLA guarantees.
DP-C: Implement a mesh network topology with multiple relay nodes; route commands and telemetry through redundant paths with dynamic rerouting on link degradation.
DP-D: Combine dual-path architecture with in-vehicle link monitoring, automatic safe-state triggers on loss, and real-time availability reporting to operators and safety systems.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a dual-path teleoperation architecture with continuous link health monitoring, automatic failover to backup path on primary failure, in-vehicle safe-state transition logic triggered by link loss timeout, and real-time availability metrics reporting to Safety Services and Site Manager dashboards.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation link availability to vehicle safe-state logic (FR-NGHE-010.2 dependency), but this is a necessary and acceptable coupling: link loss must trigger safe shutdown.
DP-D couples to network infrastructure (external to NGHE solution boundary), but this is managed via SLA and redundancy design.
DP-D couples to Safety Services for alert propagation, but this is a required functional dependency, not a design flaw.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is preferred over DP-A (single path insufficient for 0.999 target) and DP-B (SLA alone does not guarantee 0.999 for safety-critical application). DP-C (mesh) is more complex and introduces latency; DP-D is simpler and more deterministic. Coupling is acceptable because it reflects true functional interdependencies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent communication paths (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual cellular carriers) are available at the worksite.
Assumption: Network providers can deliver ≥0.9995 availability per path to achieve combined 0.999 target.
Risk: If only one path is available, DP-D cannot achieve 0.999 target; mitigation: conduct site survey before deployment.
Risk: Failover latency (time to detect failure and switch) must be <100 ms to avoid operator confusion; mitigation: implement aggressive link health polling (e.g., heartbeat every 50 ms).
Risk: Safe-state transition on link loss may interrupt critical operations; mitigation: define safe-state as 'hold last command' rather than 'full stop' where operationally acceptable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with customer: (1) acceptable failover latency, (2) definition of 'safe state' on link loss, (3) availability of dual paths at worksite, (4) acceptable downtime for maintenance of backup path. If dual paths unavailable, revise DP to DP-B with enhanced error correction and SLA guarantees, accepting lower availability target.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the dual-path command-and-telemetry link architecture, including link health monitoring, failover logic, safe-state transition triggers, and real-time availability metrics generation. Scope includes software for link state management, failover orchestration, and metrics aggregation; excludes physical network infrastructure (owned by external provider) and vehicle-side safe-state execution (owned by Vehicle module).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Tele-operator sends commands (movement, tool control, etc.) via Tasking interface; Teleoperation module receives and queues commands for transmission over dual paths.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle state (position, sensor readings, link status) and forwards to tele-operator and Site Manager dashboards via Telemetry interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module detects link loss and triggers SafetyAlert to Safety Services; Safety Services initiates safe-state transition and notifies Safety Manager.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with dual network paths (primary and backup) via Network Link interface; manages failover and monitors link health metrics from network provider.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover orchestration, and metrics generation. High containment: all link-specific logic resides here. Low diffusion: only external interfaces are to network, vehicle, and safety services (expected dependencies). This is the correct architectural home.

Safety Services could own link monitoring, but this would diffuse teleoperation-specific logic (failover, dual-path management) across two modules. Safety Services should own safe-state transition and alert propagation, not link availability mechanics. Not recommended.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-side safe-state execution and sensor telemetry, but should not own remote link management logic. Placing link availability in Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle firmware and network architecture. Not recommended.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container; link availability is a subsystem concern best owned by Teleoperation module. Assigning to NGHE Solution would bypass architectural layering. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (dual-path architecture with failover and link monitoring). Rationale: (1) Teleoperation owns the command-and-telemetry link by definition; (2) failover logic is teleoperation-specific and should not be diffused to Vehicle or Safety Services; (3) link health metrics are generated and managed within Teleoperation; (4) external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are all natural dependencies of a teleoperation module. Containment score 0.95 reflects minimal diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Failover logic may require coordination with Vehicle module (e.g., vehicle must acknowledge safe-state transition). Mitigation: define clear handshake protocol via SafetyAlert interface; Teleoperation triggers alert, Vehicle executes safe-state, Vehicle confirms via Telemetry.

Risk: Link health monitoring may require vehicle-side heartbeat generation. Mitigation: Vehicle module generates heartbeat as part of normal telemetry; Teleoperation module monitors heartbeat arrival. No additional coupling needed.

Risk: Network Link interface may be owned by external provider; Teleoperation module must abstract network-specific details. Mitigation: define Network Link interface as a contract (e.g., 'provide dual paths with failover capability'); implementation details are provider-specific.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm with architecture team: (1) Teleoperation module owns failover orchestration logic (not delegated to network provider); (2) Vehicle module owns safe-state execution and heartbeat generation; (3) Safety Services owns alert propagation and Safety Manager notification. If network provider owns failover, revise IM boundary to clarify ownership split and interface contracts.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (dual-path architecture with failover and link monitoring) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). DP-D is traceable to the refined FR and its sub-FRs (FR-010.1 through 010.4). DP-D is feasible given dual-path availability at worksite and acceptable failover latency. No FR-DP mismatch detected.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: Failover logic may leak into Vehicle module if vehicle-side safe-state execution is not cleanly separated. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert interface as a clear contract; Vehicle module responds to alert, does not implement failover logic.
Diffusion risk: Link health monitoring may require custom telemetry from vehicle. Mitigation: heartbeat is part of standard telemetry; no custom vehicle firmware needed.

Diffusion risk: Network Link interface may be ambiguous if network provider owns failover. Mitigation: clarify ownership in interface specification; if provider owns failover, Teleoperation module becomes a client of provider's failover service.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) for DP-D. All link-specific logic (dual-path management, failover orchestration, metrics generation) resides in Teleoperation. External dependencies (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are minimal and well-defined. No significant diffusion detected. IM assignment is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-010 is verifiable via continuous link state monitoring and post-evaluation statistical analysis. Teleoperation module can generate real-time availability metrics (link up/down ratio) and log them for verification. Acceptance criterion (≥0.999) is measurable and objective. Verification is feasible.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module with clear ownership. Sub-FRs are allocated as follows: FR-010.1 (establish link) -> Teleoperation; FR-010.2 (detect loss and safe-state) -> Teleoperation + Safety Services (split ownership); FR-010.3 (provide metrics) -> Teleoperation; FR-010.4 (network redundancy) -> Network provider (external). Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Clarify ownership split between Teleoperation and Safety Services for FR-010.2 (link loss detection and safe-state transition). Recommend: Teleoperation detects loss and triggers SafetyAlert; Safety Services orchestrates safe-state transition and notifies Safety Manager.

Define Network Link interface specification to clarify: (1) dual-path availability and SLA, (2) failover responsibility (Teleoperation or provider), (3) link health metrics available to Teleoperation module.
Specify failover latency requirement (e.g., <100 ms) and add as a sub-FR or design constraint to DP-D.
Define 'safe state' operationally (e.g., 'hold last command', 'full stop', 'return to home') and document in Vehicle module requirements.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (FR-010.1 through 010.4) that require further refinement and allocation. DP-D is sound and traceable to the refined FR, but its realization depends on clarifying sub-FR ownership and interface contracts. Teleoperation module is the correct IM boundary with high containment (0.95). However, sub-FRs (especially FR-010.2 and FR-010.4) require one more decomposition level to allocate to specific design elements (e.g., failover algorithm, safe-state state machine, network redundancy strategy). Recommend proceeding to next level of decomposition for each sub-FR.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle. [Decompose into: link initialization, command transmission, acknowledgment, link state tracking]

FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and initiate safe state transition within defined timeout. [Decompose into: link health monitoring, loss detection, timeout logic, safe-state trigger, Vehicle coordination]

FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link status and availability metrics to the Safety Manager and Site Manager. [Decompose into: metrics collection, real-time dashboard update, historical logging, alert generation]

FR-NGHE-010.4: The network infrastructure shall provide redundancy and failover mechanisms to achieve target availability. [Decompose into: dual-path provisioning, failover orchestration, path health monitoring, SLA enforcement]

revise.why: DP-D is sound at the system level, but requires refinement for each sub-FR. Recommend creating sub-DPs for: (1) link initialization and state tracking (DP-010.1), (2) link loss detection and safe-state trigger (DP-010.2), (3) metrics aggregation and reporting (DP-010.3), (4) dual-path failover strategy (DP-010.4). Each sub-DP should be evaluated for coupling and feasibility before proceeding to detailed design.

assumptions: Dual independent communication paths (e.g., cellular + satellite) are available at the worksite.
Network providers can deliver ≥0.9995 availability per path to achieve combined 0.999 target.
Failover latency <100 ms is acceptable for tele-operator experience.
Safe-state definition (e.g., 'hold last command') is operationally acceptable and does not violate worksite safety plan.
Vehicle module can generate heartbeat telemetry and respond to SafetyAlert within defined timeout.
Safety Services module can orchestrate safe-state transition and notify Safety Manager in real-time.
open_risks: Risk: Dual paths may not be available at all worksites; mitigation: conduct site survey before deployment; if unavailable, revise DP to single-path with enhanced error correction.
Risk: Failover latency may exceed 100 ms due to network propagation delays; mitigation: implement aggressive link health polling and pre-emptive failover logic.
Risk: Safe-state transition may interrupt critical operations; mitigation: define safe-state operationally and validate against worksite safety plan.

Risk: Link availability target (0.999) may be unachievable with available network infrastructure; mitigation: conduct feasibility study with network provider; if unachievable, negotiate revised target with customer.
Risk: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if failover logic is too complex; mitigation: implement failover as a state machine with clear transitions; test failover performance under load.
evidence_needed: Network provider SLA documentation confirming ≥0.9995 availability per path.
Worksite survey confirming dual-path availability and network topology.
Failover latency measurements from prototype or simulation.
Vehicle module specification confirming heartbeat generation and safe-state response capability.
Safety Services module specification confirming alert propagation and Safety Manager notification.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies confirming acceptability of safe-state definition.
Customer acceptance of link availability target (0.999) and evaluation period.
